artifacts.content:
To: Karen Elise Thompson-McMillan
From: Laura J. Thompson <laura.thompson48@gmail.com>
Date: January 24, 2026
Subject: Checking in (and a favor to ask)

Hi honey,

I just made my afternoon tea and thought I’d write before the rain picks up again. It’s one of those steady Gresham drizzles that makes the yard look greener but also reminds me I still haven’t called that roofer Michael recommended. The shingles over the garage at 1847 SE Willow Bend are starting to curl. I can hear your dad muttering about “deferred maintenance” in my head.

I’ve been sleeping a little better, though I’m still on the 10 mg zolpidem Dr. Karen Liu at OHSU Family Medicine renews every 30 days. She keeps reminding me that the insomnia goes all the way back to those months in the central highlands in ’68–’69, trying to sleep in sandbagged bunkers at Bastogne and Nancy with mortars dropping. Funny how a quiet house can feel louder in some ways.

Grief group at the church on Wednesdays is helping. Last week I finally said out loud that David’s heart disease and that old Agent Orange exposure near An Khe and the Ia Drang Valley were probably all tangled together. I even mentioned re‑reading that March 12, 1970 letter he sent from the surgical unit near Đức Phổ in Quảng Ngãi Province, where they accidentally shelled that hamlet and he tried to save those children. I still keep it in the desk drawer in the spare room.

Anyway, the favor: could you and Sophie maybe come out next Saturday? We could get Thai from the place you like and you can help me decide what to do with the uniforms and the Bronze Star box. I don’t want to turn the house into a museum, but I also don’t want Emily and Jacob to lose the stories.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: Checking in (and a favor to ask)
metadata.location: 1847 SE Willow Bend Drive, Gresham, OR

artifacts.content:
To: Robert "Bob" Keller
From: Laura J. Thompson <laura.thompson48@gmail.com>
Date: January 28, 2026
Subject: Talking about David with the kids

Hi Bob,

I just got off the phone with Michael. He wants to take Jacob down to the Oregon Veterans Museum in Salem next month, and he asked if I had anything of David’s that might help Jacob understand more than the usual “he was in Vietnam and the Gulf” line.

I told him about those months from February ’68 through January ’69 when David was a platoon sergeant with B Company, 2/7 Cav out of LZ Nancy near Huế, and that June 14, 1968 ambush on Highway 1 where he lost three men from his squad. You remember how he used to say that’s when the nightmares really started, the same thing he told Dr. Ellen Marks at the Portland VA back in the early 2000s.

I also mentioned his second in‑country stretch from November ’68 to August ’69 out of Phuoc Vinh, when Jimmy Dawson was killed by friendly artillery near the Cambodian border on March 22, 1969. I still have the casualty notification letter from Fort Lewis folded inside the family Bible here at the house. It’s yellowed now, but the typewriter ink is as sharp as the day it came.

Part of me wonders how much of this the grandkids really need. Emily’s doing that psychology paper down in Eugene about combat trauma, and she already knows more than I did at her age. But I also think if we don’t give them the specifics—An Khe, Da Nang, Huế, Jimmy Dawson—they’ll just get the movie version.

How are things in Boise? Has your back settled down any? Write when you can.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Talking about David with the kids

artifacts.content:
Title: Desk notebook – late night thoughts
Date: February 2, 2026
Location: Spare bedroom desk, 1847 SE Willow Bend

Couldn’t sleep again, even after the zolpidem. Rain on the roof, house too quiet without David’s snoring. Writing helps.

Things I don’t want the kids to forget, even if I never say them in group:

– My own time over there: Jan–Dec 1968 with 2/12 Cav, rotating between that fire support base west of Pleiku and the aid station. Tet ’68, working 18–20 hours at a time in that makeshift surgical tent, blood everywhere, Capt. Halpern barking orders. That May 1968 prisoner we operated on, then the trip to Long Binh to sit in a hot little JAG office and swear whether his wounds were from battle or something worse. Dr. Elaine Porter at the Portland VA finally called it PTSD in March 1974, but the label never helped me sleep.

– Second tour: Feb–Aug 1969, FSB Nancy near Kontum, about 40 air assault medevac missions. The April ’69 perimeter breach when I caught shrapnel and ended up with the Purple Heart and that 40% rating from the Vancouver VA in ’82. That’s still part of what keeps my pension and survivor benefits afloat for Michael and Karen, whether they realize it or not.

– Desert Storm later on: those nights at Al Jubayl in early ’91, tracking David’s convoy numbers in a little notebook at my bunk, checking off which runs came back after the chemical alerts.

Maybe someday I’ll turn these scribbles into a proper story for the grandkids. For now, it’s enough that I remember.

– LJT

metadata.title: Desk notebook – late night thoughts
metadata.location: 1847 SE Willow Bend Drive, Gresham, OR

artifacts.content:
To: Emily Rose Thompson
From: Grandma <laura.thompson48@gmail.com>
Date: February 6, 2026
Subject: Your paper (and a few stories)

Dear Em,

Your mom forwarded me your outline for the psychology paper. You’re braver than I was at 22, poking at all this on purpose.

You asked about “everyday” impacts, not just the big battles. For your grandpa, it wasn’t only Vietnam. From January to late March 1991 he was with that Reserve transportation unit attached to VII Corps, running 10–12 hour convoys every other night between Dhahran and staging areas near the Iraqi border. He missed your dad’s 17th birthday—February 5, 1991—sitting in a tent city in Dhahran. I wrote him a letter that week about keeping his gas mask on his lap whenever they passed the Al‑Khobar area because the intel briefing on February 19 had everyone spooked about SCUDs and chemicals.

For me, the worst wasn’t always the gore, it was the long tail. The Portland VA wrote in my 2003 exam that more than 14 months in the central highlands—sleeping in sandbagged bunkers at Bastogne and Nancy under mortar fire, hauling shattered boys into that surgical tent west of Pleiku—was the main “stressor” for my chronic insomnia. That’s why Dr. Karen Liu at OHSU still has me on that little white 10 mg pill every night.

If you mention any of this in your paper, remember these are real people, not case studies—especially those Vietnamese prisoners we operated on in May ’68 and that boy David tried to save after the accidental shelling near Đức Phổ in March 1970. You can come up next weekend and look at the letters yourself if you like.

Proud of you. Don’t forget to eat something besides coffee and instant ramen.

Love,
Grandma

Title: Thursday errands + VA clinic volunteering
Date: February 12, 2026
Time: 8:30 a.m. – 3:30 p.m.
Location: Home → East Portland VA Clinic → Gresham
Attendees: Laura

Plan for the day:

– 8:30–9:15 a.m.  Drive from 1847 SE Willow Bend to the East Portland VA clinic. Leave extra time in case of traffic on I‑84. Bring my volunteer badge, water bottle, and the little spiral notebook where I’ve been jotting questions about David’s old claims (Agent Orange exposure near An Khe/Ia Drang from May ’68–early ’69; ischemic heart disease note from Dr. Susan Patel at OHSU Cardiology back in March 2012; that awful June 1980 stressor statement he wrote about the April 17, 1969 ambush south of Marble Mountain near Da Nang).

– 9:30 a.m.–12:30 p.m.  Volunteer shift at the front desk. Greet patients, help them find the right waiting room. Try not to stare when I hear someone mention Pleiku or Khe Sanh. Remember to ask Mary at the desk if she’s ever seen anything about Desert Storm convoy exposure in the files—Dhahran, King Khalid Military City, Al Jubayl, all those chemical alarms.

– 12:30–1:00 p.m.  Quick lunch in the cafeteria. Avoid the fries. Check blood pressure if the machine in the hallway is working.

– 1:00–1:45 p.m.  Stop at credit union on the way home to double‑check that the survivor benefit deposits tied to my 40% rating from that 1982 Vancouver VA decision are still coming in correctly since David died last January.

– 2:00–3:30 p.m.  Home. Short walk around the block if it’s not pouring. Start sorting the box labeled “Al Jubayl night‑shift logs – Feb–Apr ’91 (includes David’s convoy list).”

To: Margaret "Maggie" Keller Dawson
From: Laura J. Thompson <laura.thompson48@gmail.com>
Date: February 15, 2026
Subject: Church, memories, and that old letter

Hi Mags,

I just got back from St. Luke’s. Pastor Jim did a sermon on forgiveness, which of course sent my mind straight to David. After the service he asked how I was doing with the anniversaries coming up, and we ended up revisiting what David told him back on November 15, 1998, about those four weeks on that hilltop fire base near Khe Sanh in November 1970—rockets every night, his friend killed, and the bottle becoming his best friend.

I told Pastor Jim I’d been re‑reading some of the letters again. There’s that March 12, 1970 one from the forward surgical unit near Đức Phổ in Quảng Ngãi, where David wrote about the accidental shelling of a hamlet and trying to treat those wounded children. And then the Highway 1 ambush near Huế on June 14, 1968, losing three of his boys from B Company, 2/7 Cav out of LZ Nancy. No wonder he carried so much guilt into those sessions with Dr. Marks at the Portland VA.

Funny thing is, my own ghosts look different but rhyme. I still think about Tet ’68 at that fire support base west of Pleiku, doing emergency amputations with Capt. Halpern, and later sitting in that Long Binh JAG office in May ’68 swearing under oath about a prisoner’s wounds. Then years later in Desert Storm, talking to Father Martinez in a dusty chapel tent near the Dhahran motor pool on February 23, 1991, after the SCUD hit and realizing how much it all echoed that night in October ’88 when David shoved me into the kitchen counter here on Willow Bend.

This is getting long. Just wanted you to know I’m okay, just reflective. How are things in Salem? Maybe we can meet halfway for coffee soon.

Love you,
Laura

metadata.subject: Church, memories, and that old letter

artifacts.content:
Title: Folder label + inside note – “Kids & grandkids info”
Date: February 18, 2026
Location: File drawer, spare bedroom

Outside label on manila folder:
“Kids / grandkids – important background (Michael, Karen, Emily, Jacob, Sophie)”

Inside cover note (handwritten):

If something happens to me, whoever finds this: these are things I want the kids and grandkids to have context for.

– My Vietnam service: Jan–Dec 1968 with 2/12 Cav, central highlands (FSB Bastogne, fire base west of Pleiku), then Feb–Aug 1969 out of FSB Nancy near Kontum. More than 14 months in combat zones, sleeping in sandbagged bunkers, constant mortar fire. Portland VA later said this was the main cause of my chronic insomnia; Dr. Karen Liu at OHSU Family Medicine keeps renewing my nightly zolpidem.

– Purple Heart & rating: Wounded by shrapnel during an April 1969 perimeter breach at Nancy. That led to the Purple Heart and a 40% service‑connected disability from the Vancouver, WA VA office in 1982, which affects pension and survivor benefits (Social Security, etc.).

– Desert Storm: Deployed again Nov 1990–Apr 1991 (Dhahran, King Khalid Military City, Al Jubayl). Night‑shift NCO at Al Jubayl tracking fuel and ammo convoys, including David’s truck numbers. Kept separate handwritten log (see box in attic labeled “Al Jubayl night‑shift logs – Feb–Apr ’91 (includes David’s convoy list)”).

– David’s service: Multiple tours with 1st Cav (An Khe, Da Nang, Huế, Phuoc Vinh, Khe Sanh). Letters from Đức Phổ (March 12, 1970 hamlet shelling) and about Jimmy Dawson’s friendly‑fire death near the Cambodian border (March 22, 1969) are in the smaller envelope.

Signed,
Laura J. Thompson
1/18/2026

metadata.title: Kids & grandkids info – folder note
metadata.location: 1847 SE Willow Bend Drive, Gresham, OR
